summary:
The ancient Egyptians developed one of the world's earliest solar calendars, likely during the late fourth or early third millennium BC. Observing that the annual flooding of the Nile coincided with the heliacal rising of the star Sirius, Egyptian astronomers constructed a 365-day civil calendar divided into three seasons of four months each, with each month containing thirty days and five additional intercalary days added at the year's end. This calendar provided the administrative backbone for planning agricultural cycles, religious festivals, and state taxation.

The Egyptian solar calendar is distinguished from earlier lunar-based systems by its fixed annual length, which made it far more practical for long-term record-keeping and planning. Although it accumulated a small drift against the true solar year over centuries — lacking the leap-year correction — its logical structure influenced later calendar systems. The Roman Julian calendar, introduced in 46 BC, drew directly on Egyptian precedent in adopting a 365-day framework, demonstrating the enduring impact of this early Egyptian innovation.
